operating_state: Deere achieved record net sales of $52.6 billion in fiscal 2022, representing 19% growth driven by strong demand across all segments with price realization offsetting negative currency translation impacts. Equipment backlogs remained elevated though Small Agriculture and Turf backlog decreased from prior year levels. The company expanded technology capabilities through new offices in Chicago and Austin plus strategic acquisitions including majority ownership in Kreisel Electric for battery technology and GUSS Automation for autonomous orchard systems.
cost_structure: Cost of sales ratio increased to 73.7% from 73.3% primarily due to elevated material costs, supply chain disruptions causing expedited freight charges, and production inefficiencies from higher partially completed inventory levels. Research and development expenses rose 20% reflecting continued technology investments in autonomous systems and precision agriculture solutions. Administrative expenses increased driven by higher credit loss provisions, merit pay increases, and UAW ratification bonuses.
material_events: The company completed acquisition of full ownership in the Deere-Hitachi joint venture for $690 million, generating a $326 million non-cash gain on previously held equity investment. Share repurchases totaled $3.1 billion in the fourth quarter with an additional $18.0 billion repurchase program authorized in December 2022. The UAW strike from October to November 2021 affected operations at 14 facilities, followed by a new six-year collective bargaining agreement with immediate 10% wage increase. Operations suspended shipments to Russia with dealer agreements terminated as of November 2022.
macro_exposures: Supply chain disruptions from industry capacity constraints, material availability, and global logistics delays affected operations with raw material price volatility expected to persist into 2023. The Russia-Ukraine conflict created $255 million pretax expense including asset impairments and inventory reserves with net exposure totaling approximately $266 million. Rising Federal Reserve interest rates since March 2022 compressed financial services spreads with further compression anticipated as rate increases impact borrowings faster than financing receivables.
forward_outlook: North American large agricultural machinery industry sales forecasted to increase 5 to 10 percent in 2023, while small agricultural and turf equipment expected flat to down 5 percent, with European agricultural machinery sales projected flat to up 5 percent. Management expects to sell more large agricultural equipment in 2023 than 2022 across major regions with order books full through most of 2023. The company is executing Smart Industrial operating model transformation with Leap Ambitions framework targeting goals by 2026-2030.
uncertainty_profile: Achievement of Leap Ambitions faces execution risks including customer adoption rates of precision technology, availability of quality battery cells, and infrastructure development timelines. The company faces ongoing antitrust class action lawsuits alleging monopolization of agricultural equipment repair services markets, while right-to-repair legislation proposals could require disclosure of embedded software code. Credit loss allowances increased to $361 million from $207 million primarily due to economic uncertainty, while ongoing supply chain volatility and cybersecurity threats to connected equipment pose operational risks.
